items.q: What attitude does Pop Art take toward consumer culture?
items.correct: Ambiguous - it both celebrates and critiques mass media imagery.
items.why: Students read Pop Art as either pure celebration or pure protest. Warhol's deadpan repetition of Marilyn or soup cans is deliberately AMBIGUOUS, leaving viewers to decide if it's homage or critique.

items.correct: Art that does not depict any recognisable object - exploring form, colour, and line directly.
items.misconception: Art that looks 'a bit like' something but not exactly.
items.random_wrong: Art that doesn't represent any country.
items.why: Students soften the term to 'stylised'. Non-representational means NO reference to objects at all - pure visual exploration (Rothko, Mondrian), distinct from semi-abstract work that simplifies recognisable forms.

items.correct: Removing material from a solid block to reveal the form (e.g., carving stone).
items.why: Students confuse 'subtractive' with arithmetic. In sculpture, additive = building up (clay, welding); subtractive = taking away (carving). Michelangelo described 'freeing' figures from the marble - quintessential subtractive thinking.

items.q: Which technique uses crossing lines for tone?
items.why: It's Cross-hatching. “Stippling” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
It's Dots. “Lines” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many primary colours are commonly used in pigment mixing?
items.why: The real figure is 3 colours. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
